The wo_skills agent discovered the same gaps through manual exploration (reading files, running the check-translations.js script at Step 22), taking 61 steps vs 52 steps. Both agents correctly identified all gaps, preserved existing files, created ko.ts with Korean content, updated index.ts with all three changes, and completed ko.json with parts/descriptions/interactive sections. The quality of outputs is essentially equivalent in this case, suggesting the baseline agent was capable enough to independently discover the project structure without the skill. However, wi_skills was more efficient (9 fewer steps) and followed a more systematic approach from the outset rather than through exploration.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced functionally equivalent outputs — all 6 judge items pass for both runs. Both agents correctly created all required files (de.json, de.ts, index.ts update, three MDX files), registered the locale with all three required index.ts changes, and produced natural German translations. Notably, the wo_skills agent actually produced slightly higher quality German text by using proper Unicode umlauts (ü, ö, ä, ß) throughout, whereas the wi_skills agent used ASCII substitutes (ue, oe, ae) for most umlauts. The wo_skills agent also partially translated code comments/strings inside code blocks (which could be argued as a minor negative for J1.2, but the JSX tags were preserved). In this particular test case, the wo_skills baseline agent performed comparably to the wi_skills agent, achieving all integration steps without the skill's guidance. The skill provided a more structured approach but did not result in a measurably superior outcome for this scenario. Overall quality: essentially equivalent, with wo_skills having a slight edge in German text quality due to proper umlaut usage.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully. Note: In wi_skills, the skill sub-agent completed all integration steps internally before the outer agent audited, causing the status report to inaccurately present all components as pre-existing rather than identifying which were originally missing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both runs achieved full verifier pass (6/6) and completed the technical integration identically. However, on the key qualitative dimension of J3.1 (audit accuracy), wo_skills performed better: its status report correctly identified the three pre-existing gaps (missing 23 MDX files, missing ko.ts, incomplete ko.json), satisfying the evaluation criterion. In contrast, wi_skills delegated all work to a sub-agent via the /book-translation skill invocation, which completed everything before the outer agent audited the state, causing the status report to falsely claim everything was already present. This is an ironic outcome: the skill caused the agent to lose visibility into what was actually missing. For J3.2 through J3.6, both runs produced equivalent quality output. Overall, wo_skills produced a more accurate and useful status report (J3.1 pass vs fail), while wi_skills produced identical technical artifacts but a misleading audit. wo_skills scores 5/6 on judge items; wi_skills scores 4/6.
